title: Adult Sepsis Recognition and Management Pathway (v1.0, 2014)
pathway_title: Adult Sepsis Recognition and Management Pathway
issuing_body: Meridian Health Network — Clinical Standards Office (SYNTHETIC)
cross_reference_titles: Antimicrobial Stewardship Pathway
Acute Kidney Injury (AKI) Detection and Management Pathway

sections.heading: Scope
sections.text: This pathway covers recognition and first-hour management of suspected sepsis in adults aged 18 and above presenting to the Emergency Department or inpatient wards.

sections.heading: Recognition
sections.text: Screen any patient with suspected infection using SIRS criteria: temperature above 38C or below 36C, heart rate above 90, respiratory rate above 20, white cell count abnormal. Two or more criteria with suspected infection should trigger a sepsis alert.

sections.heading: Sepsis Six Bundle
sections.text: Within one hour of recognition, deliver: (1) high-flow oxygen, (2) blood cultures, (3) intravenous broad-spectrum antibiotics, (4) intravenous fluid challenge, (5) measure serum lactate, (6) measure hourly urine output.

sections.heading: Antibiotics
sections.text: Start empirical broad-spectrum antibiotics within one hour. For specific antibiotic choice and dose adjustment in kidney impairment, follow the Antimicrobial Stewardship Pathway.

sections.heading: Fluids
sections.text: Give an initial crystalloid bolus of 30 mL/kg for hypotension or lactate above 4 mmol/L. Reassess after each bolus.

sections.heading: Escalation
sections.text: If lactate remains above 4 mmol/L or hypotension persists after fluids, escalate to critical care for consideration of vasopressors.

content:
## Scope
This pathway covers recognition and first-hour management of suspected sepsis in adults aged 18 and above presenting to the Emergency Department or inpatient wards.

## Recognition
Screen any patient with suspected infection using SIRS criteria: temperature above 38C or below 36C, heart rate above 90, respiratory rate above 20, white cell count abnormal. Two or more criteria with suspected infection should trigger a sepsis alert.

## Sepsis Six Bundle
Within one hour of recognition, deliver: (1) high-flow oxygen, (2) blood cultures, (3) intravenous broad-spectrum antibiotics, (4) intravenous fluid challenge, (5) measure serum lactate, (6) measure hourly urine output.

## Antibiotics
Start empirical broad-spectrum antibiotics within one hour. For specific antibiotic choice and dose adjustment in kidney impairment, follow the Antimicrobial Stewardship Pathway.

## Fluids
Give an initial crystalloid bolus of 30 mL/kg for hypotension or lactate above 4 mmol/L. Reassess after each bolus.

## Escalation
If lactate remains above 4 mmol/L or hypotension persists after fluids, escalate to critical care for consideration of vasopressors.
citation: Adult Sepsis Recognition and Management Pathway v1.0 (2014), Meridian Health Network — Clinical Standards Office (SYNTHETIC)

title: Adult Sepsis Recognition and Management Pathway (v2.0, 2019)
pathway_title: Adult Sepsis Recognition and Management Pathway
issuing_body: Meridian Health Network — Clinical Standards Office (SYNTHETIC)
cross_reference_titles: Antimicrobial Stewardship Pathway
Acute Kidney Injury (AKI) Detection and Management Pathway
Chronic Heart Failure Management Pathway

sections.heading: Scope
sections.text: This version replaces SIRS-based screening with qSOFA and the National Early Warning Score 2 (NEWS2) for adults with suspected infection.

sections.heading: Recognition
sections.text: Use qSOFA: respiratory rate 22 or above, altered mentation, systolic blood pressure 100 mmHg or below. A qSOFA score of 2 or more indicates higher risk. NEWS2 of 5 or more should also trigger a sepsis screen.

sections.heading: Sepsis Six Bundle
sections.text: The Sepsis Six bundle is retained and must still be completed within one hour of recognition. Lactate is now mandatory at recognition and repeated at 2 hours if initially raised.

sections.heading: Antibiotics
sections.text: Empirical antibiotics within one hour remain the standard. Renal dose adjustment and de-escalation at 48-72 hours are governed by the Antimicrobial Stewardship Pathway.

sections.heading: Fluids
sections.text: Initial bolus reduced to 250-500 mL aliquots with reassessment, up to 30 mL/kg, to avoid fluid overload in heart failure and kidney patients. See Acute Kidney Injury Pathway for fluid caution.

sections.heading: Escalation
sections.text: Persistent lactate above 2 mmol/L after resuscitation now defines septic shock when vasopressors are required to keep mean arterial pressure at 65 mmHg or above.

content:
## Scope
This version replaces SIRS-based screening with qSOFA and the National Early Warning Score 2 (NEWS2) for adults with suspected infection.

## Recognition
Use qSOFA: respiratory rate 22 or above, altered mentation, systolic blood pressure 100 mmHg or below. A qSOFA score of 2 or more indicates higher risk. NEWS2 of 5 or more should also trigger a sepsis screen.

## Sepsis Six Bundle
The Sepsis Six bundle is retained and must still be completed within one hour of recognition. Lactate is now mandatory at recognition and repeated at 2 hours if initially raised.

## Antibiotics
Empirical antibiotics within one hour remain the standard. Renal dose adjustment and de-escalation at 48-72 hours are governed by the Antimicrobial Stewardship Pathway.

## Fluids
Initial bolus reduced to 250-500 mL aliquots with reassessment, up to 30 mL/kg, to avoid fluid overload in heart failure and kidney patients. See Acute Kidney Injury Pathway for fluid caution.

## Escalation
Persistent lactate above 2 mmol/L after resuscitation now defines septic shock when vasopressors are required to keep mean arterial pressure at 65 mmHg or above.
citation: Adult Sepsis Recognition and Management Pathway v2.0 (2019), Meridian Health Network — Clinical Standards Office (SYNTHETIC)

title: Adult Sepsis Recognition and Management Pathway (v3.0, 2024)
pathway_title: Adult Sepsis Recognition and Management Pathway
issuing_body: Meridian Health Network — Clinical Standards Office (SYNTHETIC)
cross_reference_titles: Antimicrobial Stewardship Pathway
Acute Kidney Injury (AKI) Detection and Management Pathway
Pre-eclampsia and Maternal Deterioration Pathway

sections.heading: Scope
sections.text: Current version. Adds a dedicated section on sepsis in pregnancy and links to the Maternal Sepsis annex of the Pre-eclampsia and Maternal Deterioration Pathway.

sections.heading: Recognition
sections.text: NEWS2 remains the primary inpatient trigger. For maternal patients use MEOWS (Modified Early Obstetric Warning Score) because normal pregnancy physiology alters vital sign thresholds.

sections.heading: Sepsis Six Bundle
sections.text: Bundle unchanged but documentation must record the exact recognition timestamp to measure the one-hour target. Lactate point-of-care testing is preferred.

sections.heading: Antibiotics
sections.text: First dose within one hour. For penicillin allergy and for renal dosing, defer to the Antimicrobial Stewardship Pathway current version.

sections.heading: Fluids
sections.text: Balanced crystalloid preferred over 0.9% saline. Continue 250-500 mL aliquots with reassessment. In acute kidney injury, follow fluid-balance guidance in the Acute Kidney Injury Pathway.

sections.heading: Escalation
sections.text: Septic shock definition unchanged. Maternal sepsis with deterioration must trigger a senior obstetric and critical care review per the Maternal Deterioration Pathway.

content:
## Scope
Current version. Adds a dedicated section on sepsis in pregnancy and links to the Maternal Sepsis annex of the Pre-eclampsia and Maternal Deterioration Pathway.

## Recognition
NEWS2 remains the primary inpatient trigger. For maternal patients use MEOWS (Modified Early Obstetric Warning Score) because normal pregnancy physiology alters vital sign thresholds.

## Sepsis Six Bundle
Bundle unchanged but documentation must record the exact recognition timestamp to measure the one-hour target. Lactate point-of-care testing is preferred.

## Antibiotics
First dose within one hour. For penicillin allergy and for renal dosing, defer to the Antimicrobial Stewardship Pathway current version.

## Fluids
Balanced crystalloid preferred over 0.9% saline. Continue 250-500 mL aliquots with reassessment. In acute kidney injury, follow fluid-balance guidance in the Acute Kidney Injury Pathway.

## Escalation
Septic shock definition unchanged. Maternal sepsis with deterioration must trigger a senior obstetric and critical care review per the Maternal Deterioration Pathway.
citation: Adult Sepsis Recognition and Management Pathway v3.0 (2024), Meridian Health Network — Clinical Standards Office (SYNTHETIC)

title: Antimicrobial Stewardship Pathway (v1.0, 2015)
issuing_body: Meridian Health Network — Clinical Standards Office (SYNTHETIC)
cross_reference_titles: Acute Kidney Injury (AKI) Detection and Management Pathway

sections.heading: Scope
sections.text: Governs empirical antibiotic selection, duration, renal dose adjustment, and de-escalation across the network.

sections.heading: Empirical Choice
sections.text: For undifferentiated severe infection start piperacillin-tazobactam. For suspected meningitis add ceftriaxone. Always send cultures before the first dose where it does not delay treatment beyond one hour.

sections.heading: Renal Dose Adjustment
sections.text: Estimate kidney function using eGFR. Reduce dose or extend interval for renally cleared agents when eGFR is below 30 mL/min. The Acute Kidney Injury Pathway defines how to detect falling eGFR.

sections.heading: Penicillin Allergy
sections.text: For confirmed severe penicillin allergy use a non-beta-lactam regimen such as ciprofloxacin plus metronidazole.

sections.heading: De-escalation
sections.text: Review all empirical antibiotics at 48-72 hours against culture results and narrow the spectrum.

sections.heading: Duration
sections.text: Default course is 5-7 days for most infections unless a deep-seated source requires longer.

content:
## Scope
Governs empirical antibiotic selection, duration, renal dose adjustment, and de-escalation across the network.

## Empirical Choice
For undifferentiated severe infection start piperacillin-tazobactam. For suspected meningitis add ceftriaxone. Always send cultures before the first dose where it does not delay treatment beyond one hour.

## Renal Dose Adjustment
Estimate kidney function using eGFR. Reduce dose or extend interval for renally cleared agents when eGFR is below 30 mL/min. The Acute Kidney Injury Pathway defines how to detect falling eGFR.

## Penicillin Allergy
For confirmed severe penicillin allergy use a non-beta-lactam regimen such as ciprofloxacin plus metronidazole.

## De-escalation
Review all empirical antibiotics at 48-72 hours against culture results and narrow the spectrum.

## Duration
Default course is 5-7 days for most infections unless a deep-seated source requires longer.
citation: Antimicrobial Stewardship Pathway v1.0 (2015), Meridian Health Network — Clinical Standards Office (SYNTHETIC)

title: Antimicrobial Stewardship Pathway (v2.0, 2021)
issuing_body: Meridian Health Network — Clinical Standards Office (SYNTHETIC)
cross_reference_titles: Acute Kidney Injury (AKI) Detection and Management Pathway
Adult Sepsis Recognition and Management Pathway

sections.heading: Scope
sections.text: Updated to align with rising carbapenem resistance and to add explicit sepsis cross-links.

sections.heading: Empirical Choice
sections.text: First-line for severe sepsis of unknown source remains piperacillin-tazobactam. Reserve meropenem for known extended-spectrum beta-lactamase risk or recent failure of first-line therapy.

sections.heading: Renal Dose Adjustment
sections.text: Use eGFR bands: 30-50, 15-29, and below 15 mL/min, each with specific dose tables for piperacillin-tazobactam, vancomycin, and gentamicin. Recheck eGFR daily during acute kidney injury.

sections.heading: Penicillin Allergy
sections.text: Adopt allergy de-labelling: many reported penicillin allergies are not true allergies. A pharmacist-led assessment can release first-line agents safely.

sections.heading: De-escalation
sections.text: Mandatory antibiotic review at 48 hours documented in the electronic record, with stop, switch to oral, or continue decision.

sections.heading: Duration
sections.text: Move to 5 days for uncomplicated infections in line with shorter-course evidence.

content:
## Scope
Updated to align with rising carbapenem resistance and to add explicit sepsis cross-links.

## Empirical Choice
First-line for severe sepsis of unknown source remains piperacillin-tazobactam. Reserve meropenem for known extended-spectrum beta-lactamase risk or recent failure of first-line therapy.

## Renal Dose Adjustment
Use eGFR bands: 30-50, 15-29, and below 15 mL/min, each with specific dose tables for piperacillin-tazobactam, vancomycin, and gentamicin. Recheck eGFR daily during acute kidney injury.

## Penicillin Allergy
Adopt allergy de-labelling: many reported penicillin allergies are not true allergies. A pharmacist-led assessment can release first-line agents safely.

## De-escalation
Mandatory antibiotic review at 48 hours documented in the electronic record, with stop, switch to oral, or continue decision.

## Duration
Move to 5 days for uncomplicated infections in line with shorter-course evidence.
citation: Antimicrobial Stewardship Pathway v2.0 (2021), Meridian Health Network — Clinical Standards Office (SYNTHETIC)

title: Antimicrobial Stewardship Pathway (v3.0, 2025)
issuing_body: Meridian Health Network — Clinical Standards Office (SYNTHETIC)
cross_reference_titles: Acute Kidney Injury (AKI) Detection and Management Pathway
Adult Sepsis Recognition and Management Pathway
Pre-eclampsia and Maternal Deterioration Pathway

sections.heading: Scope
sections.text: Current version. Adds outpatient parenteral antibiotic therapy (OPAT) and a maternal antibiotics annex.

sections.heading: Empirical Choice
sections.text: Unchanged first-line piperacillin-tazobactam for severe sepsis. New guidance: for maternal sepsis avoid agents contraindicated in pregnancy and coordinate with the Maternal Deterioration Pathway.

sections.heading: Renal Dose Adjustment
sections.text: eGFR bands retained. Vancomycin now requires area-under-curve monitoring rather than trough-only. During acute kidney injury follow daily eGFR checks per the Acute Kidney Injury Pathway.

sections.heading: Penicillin Allergy
sections.text: Pharmacist-led de-labelling expanded network-wide. Confirmed severe allergy regimen unchanged.

sections.heading: De-escalation
sections.text: 48-hour review unchanged. Add procalcitonin-guided stopping for respiratory infections where available.

sections.heading: Duration
sections.text: Default 5 days. OPAT pathway allows safe completion of longer courses at home with monitoring.

content:
## Scope
Current version. Adds outpatient parenteral antibiotic therapy (OPAT) and a maternal antibiotics annex.

## Empirical Choice
Unchanged first-line piperacillin-tazobactam for severe sepsis. New guidance: for maternal sepsis avoid agents contraindicated in pregnancy and coordinate with the Maternal Deterioration Pathway.

## Renal Dose Adjustment
eGFR bands retained. Vancomycin now requires area-under-curve monitoring rather than trough-only. During acute kidney injury follow daily eGFR checks per the Acute Kidney Injury Pathway.

## Penicillin Allergy
Pharmacist-led de-labelling expanded network-wide. Confirmed severe allergy regimen unchanged.

## De-escalation
48-hour review unchanged. Add procalcitonin-guided stopping for respiratory infections where available.

## Duration
Default 5 days. OPAT pathway allows safe completion of longer courses at home with monitoring.
citation: Antimicrobial Stewardship Pathway v3.0 (2025), Meridian Health Network — Clinical Standards Office (SYNTHETIC)

title: Acute Kidney Injury (AKI) Detection and Management Pathway (v1.0, 2016)
pathway_title: Acute Kidney Injury (AKI) Detection and Management Pathway
issuing_body: Meridian Health Network — Clinical Standards Office (SYNTHETIC)
cross_reference_titles: Adult Sepsis Recognition and Management Pathway
Antimicrobial Stewardship Pathway

sections.heading: Scope
sections.text: Detection, staging, and early management of acute kidney injury in adults.

sections.heading: Detection and Staging
sections.text: Stage AKI using KDIGO creatinine criteria: Stage 1 is a 1.5-1.9 times rise in serum creatinine, Stage 2 is 2.0-2.9 times, Stage 3 is 3.0 times or more, or initiation of dialysis.

sections.heading: Common Causes
sections.text: Most inpatient AKI is pre-renal from hypovolaemia or sepsis. Review nephrotoxic drugs and contrast exposure.

sections.heading: Management
sections.text: Treat the cause, optimise fluid balance, and stop nephrotoxic drugs. For sepsis-related AKI, resuscitate per the Adult Sepsis Pathway while avoiding fluid overload.

sections.heading: Drug Dosing
sections.text: Falling eGFR requires antibiotic and analgesic dose review. The Antimicrobial Stewardship Pathway holds the renal dose tables.

sections.heading: Referral
sections.text: Refer to nephrology for Stage 3 AKI, suspected intrinsic disease, or need for renal replacement therapy.

content:
## Scope
Detection, staging, and early management of acute kidney injury in adults.

## Detection and Staging
Stage AKI using KDIGO creatinine criteria: Stage 1 is a 1.5-1.9 times rise in serum creatinine, Stage 2 is 2.0-2.9 times, Stage 3 is 3.0 times or more, or initiation of dialysis.

## Common Causes
Most inpatient AKI is pre-renal from hypovolaemia or sepsis. Review nephrotoxic drugs and contrast exposure.

## Management
Treat the cause, optimise fluid balance, and stop nephrotoxic drugs. For sepsis-related AKI, resuscitate per the Adult Sepsis Pathway while avoiding fluid overload.

## Drug Dosing
Falling eGFR requires antibiotic and analgesic dose review. The Antimicrobial Stewardship Pathway holds the renal dose tables.

## Referral
Refer to nephrology for Stage 3 AKI, suspected intrinsic disease, or need for renal replacement therapy.
citation: Acute Kidney Injury (AKI) Detection and Management Pathway v1.0 (2016), Meridian Health Network — Clinical Standards Office (SYNTHETIC)

title: Acute Kidney Injury (AKI) Detection and Management Pathway (v2.0, 2022)
pathway_title: Acute Kidney Injury (AKI) Detection and Management Pathway
issuing_body: Meridian Health Network — Clinical Standards Office (SYNTHETIC)
cross_reference_titles: Adult Sepsis Recognition and Management Pathway
Antimicrobial Stewardship Pathway
Chronic Heart Failure Management Pathway

sections.heading: Scope
sections.text: Adds an automated electronic AKI alert driven by laboratory creatinine trends.

sections.heading: Detection and Staging
sections.text: KDIGO staging retained and now also uses urine output criteria: less than 0.5 mL/kg/hour for 6 hours signals Stage 1. The electronic alert fires automatically on a qualifying creatinine rise.

sections.heading: Common Causes
sections.text: Pre-renal causes still dominate. Add immune checkpoint inhibitor nephritis to the differential for oncology patients.

sections.heading: Management
sections.text: Fluid strategy now emphasises balanced crystalloid and careful assessment to avoid overload, especially in heart failure (see Heart Failure Pathway). Stop nephrotoxins and review contrast.

sections.heading: Drug Dosing
sections.text: Daily eGFR review during AKI. Vancomycin and gentamicin require level monitoring; see the Antimicrobial Stewardship Pathway dose bands.

sections.heading: Referral
sections.text: Stage 3 AKI, refractory hyperkalaemia, severe acidosis, or fluid overload unresponsive to treatment require urgent nephrology and critical care review.

content:
## Scope
Adds an automated electronic AKI alert driven by laboratory creatinine trends.

## Detection and Staging
KDIGO staging retained and now also uses urine output criteria: less than 0.5 mL/kg/hour for 6 hours signals Stage 1. The electronic alert fires automatically on a qualifying creatinine rise.

## Common Causes
Pre-renal causes still dominate. Add immune checkpoint inhibitor nephritis to the differential for oncology patients.

## Management
Fluid strategy now emphasises balanced crystalloid and careful assessment to avoid overload, especially in heart failure (see Heart Failure Pathway). Stop nephrotoxins and review contrast.

## Drug Dosing
Daily eGFR review during AKI. Vancomycin and gentamicin require level monitoring; see the Antimicrobial Stewardship Pathway dose bands.

## Referral
Stage 3 AKI, refractory hyperkalaemia, severe acidosis, or fluid overload unresponsive to treatment require urgent nephrology and critical care review.
citation: Acute Kidney Injury (AKI) Detection and Management Pathway v2.0 (2022), Meridian Health Network — Clinical Standards Office (SYNTHETIC)

title: Chronic Heart Failure Management Pathway (v1.0, 2015)
issuing_body: Meridian Health Network — Clinical Standards Office (SYNTHETIC)
cross_reference_titles: Acute Kidney Injury (AKI) Detection and Management Pathway
Adult Sepsis Recognition and Management Pathway

sections.heading: Scope
sections.text: Diagnosis and long-term management of chronic heart failure with reduced ejection fraction in adults.

sections.heading: Diagnosis
sections.text: Measure NT-proBNP. A level above 2000 ng/L warrants urgent echocardiography within 2 weeks.

sections.heading: First-line Therapy
sections.text: Start an ACE inhibitor and a beta-blocker, titrating to target doses as tolerated.

sections.heading: Fluid Caution
sections.text: Patients are sensitive to fluid overload. When such patients develop sepsis or AKI, fluid resuscitation must be cautious and closely monitored.

sections.heading: Monitoring
sections.text: Check kidney function and potassium within 1-2 weeks of starting or increasing ACE inhibitors.

content:
## Scope
Diagnosis and long-term management of chronic heart failure with reduced ejection fraction in adults.

## Diagnosis
Measure NT-proBNP. A level above 2000 ng/L warrants urgent echocardiography within 2 weeks.

## First-line Therapy
Start an ACE inhibitor and a beta-blocker, titrating to target doses as tolerated.

## Fluid Caution
Patients are sensitive to fluid overload. When such patients develop sepsis or AKI, fluid resuscitation must be cautious and closely monitored.

## Monitoring
Check kidney function and potassium within 1-2 weeks of starting or increasing ACE inhibitors.
citation: Chronic Heart Failure Management Pathway v1.0 (2015), Meridian Health Network — Clinical Standards Office (SYNTHETIC)

title: Chronic Heart Failure Management Pathway (v2.0, 2023)
issuing_body: Meridian Health Network — Clinical Standards Office (SYNTHETIC)
cross_reference_titles: Acute Kidney Injury (AKI) Detection and Management Pathway
Adult Sepsis Recognition and Management Pathway

sections.heading: Scope
sections.text: Updated to four-pillar therapy for heart failure with reduced ejection fraction.

sections.heading: Diagnosis
sections.text: NT-proBNP thresholds retained. Echocardiography remains the confirmatory test.

sections.heading: Four-Pillar Therapy
sections.text: Combine an ARNI or ACE inhibitor, a beta-blocker, a mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist, and an SGLT2 inhibitor. Introduce all four early and titrate.

sections.heading: Fluid Caution
sections.text: Fluid overload risk persists. Sepsis resuscitation should use small aliquots with reassessment; coordinate with the Adult Sepsis Pathway and Acute Kidney Injury Pathway.

sections.heading: Monitoring
sections.text: Monitor kidney function and potassium after starting mineralocorticoid receptor antagonists and SGLT2 inhibitors.

content:
## Scope
Updated to four-pillar therapy for heart failure with reduced ejection fraction.

## Diagnosis
NT-proBNP thresholds retained. Echocardiography remains the confirmatory test.

## Four-Pillar Therapy
Combine an ARNI or ACE inhibitor, a beta-blocker, a mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist, and an SGLT2 inhibitor. Introduce all four early and titrate.

## Fluid Caution
Fluid overload risk persists. Sepsis resuscitation should use small aliquots with reassessment; coordinate with the Adult Sepsis Pathway and Acute Kidney Injury Pathway.

## Monitoring
Monitor kidney function and potassium after starting mineralocorticoid receptor antagonists and SGLT2 inhibitors.
citation: Chronic Heart Failure Management Pathway v2.0 (2023), Meridian Health Network — Clinical Standards Office (SYNTHETIC)

title: Type 2 Diabetes Inpatient and Outpatient Pathway (v1.0, 2014)
pathway_title: Type 2 Diabetes Inpatient and Outpatient Pathway
issuing_body: Meridian Health Network — Clinical Standards Office (SYNTHETIC)
cross_reference_titles: Acute Kidney Injury (AKI) Detection and Management Pathway

sections.heading: Scope
sections.text: Glycaemic management of adults with type 2 diabetes across inpatient and outpatient settings.

sections.heading: Diagnosis
sections.text: Diagnose with HbA1c of 48 mmol/mol (6.5%) or above, confirmed on repeat unless symptomatic with high glucose.

sections.heading: First-line
sections.text: Start metformin unless contraindicated by kidney function. Lifestyle advice for all.

sections.heading: Renal Caution
sections.text: Metformin must be reviewed if eGFR falls. Stop metformin if eGFR is below 30 mL/min. Detection of falling eGFR follows the Acute Kidney Injury Pathway.

sections.heading: Targets
sections.text: Aim for an individualised HbA1c target, commonly 53 mmol/mol (7%).

content:
## Scope
Glycaemic management of adults with type 2 diabetes across inpatient and outpatient settings.

## Diagnosis
Diagnose with HbA1c of 48 mmol/mol (6.5%) or above, confirmed on repeat unless symptomatic with high glucose.

## First-line
Start metformin unless contraindicated by kidney function. Lifestyle advice for all.

## Renal Caution
Metformin must be reviewed if eGFR falls. Stop metformin if eGFR is below 30 mL/min. Detection of falling eGFR follows the Acute Kidney Injury Pathway.

## Targets
Aim for an individualised HbA1c target, commonly 53 mmol/mol (7%).
citation: Type 2 Diabetes Inpatient and Outpatient Pathway v1.0 (2014), Meridian Health Network — Clinical Standards Office (SYNTHETIC)

title: Type 2 Diabetes Inpatient and Outpatient Pathway (v2.0, 2020)
pathway_title: Type 2 Diabetes Inpatient and Outpatient Pathway
issuing_body: Meridian Health Network — Clinical Standards Office (SYNTHETIC)
cross_reference_titles: Acute Kidney Injury (AKI) Detection and Management Pathway
Chronic Heart Failure Management Pathway

sections.heading: Scope
sections.text: Adds cardiovascular and renal protective drug classes.

sections.heading: Diagnosis
sections.text: Diagnostic thresholds unchanged.

sections.heading: First-line
sections.text: Metformin remains first-line. Add an SGLT2 inhibitor early for patients with established cardiovascular disease, heart failure, or chronic kidney disease.

sections.heading: Renal Caution
sections.text: Continue to review metformin against eGFR. SGLT2 inhibitors also have eGFR thresholds for initiation. Use the Acute Kidney Injury Pathway for eGFR monitoring during illness.

sections.heading: Sick Day Rules
sections.text: During acute illness, advise temporary stopping of metformin and SGLT2 inhibitors to reduce the risk of lactic acidosis and diabetic ketoacidosis.

sections.heading: Targets
sections.text: Targets individualised; relax for frail older adults.

content:
## Scope
Adds cardiovascular and renal protective drug classes.

## Diagnosis
Diagnostic thresholds unchanged.

## First-line
Metformin remains first-line. Add an SGLT2 inhibitor early for patients with established cardiovascular disease, heart failure, or chronic kidney disease.

## Renal Caution
Continue to review metformin against eGFR. SGLT2 inhibitors also have eGFR thresholds for initiation. Use the Acute Kidney Injury Pathway for eGFR monitoring during illness.

## Sick Day Rules
During acute illness, advise temporary stopping of metformin and SGLT2 inhibitors to reduce the risk of lactic acidosis and diabetic ketoacidosis.

## Targets
Targets individualised; relax for frail older adults.
citation: Type 2 Diabetes Inpatient and Outpatient Pathway v2.0 (2020), Meridian Health Network — Clinical Standards Office (SYNTHETIC)

title: Type 2 Diabetes Inpatient and Outpatient Pathway (v3.0, 2025)
pathway_title: Type 2 Diabetes Inpatient and Outpatient Pathway
issuing_body: Meridian Health Network — Clinical Standards Office (SYNTHETIC)
cross_reference_titles: Acute Kidney Injury (AKI) Detection and Management Pathway
Chronic Heart Failure Management Pathway

sections.heading: Scope
sections.text: Current version. Adds GLP-1 receptor agonists for weight and cardiovascular benefit.

sections.heading: Diagnosis
sections.text: Unchanged.

sections.heading: First-line
sections.text: Metformin plus early SGLT2 inhibitor where indicated. Consider a GLP-1 receptor agonist for obesity or high cardiovascular risk.

sections.heading: Renal Caution
sections.text: eGFR-based review unchanged. During acute kidney injury, hold SGLT2 inhibitors and metformin per sick day rules and the Acute Kidney Injury Pathway.

sections.heading: Sick Day Rules
sections.text: Reinforced. Provide every patient a written sick day rules card.

sections.heading: Targets
sections.text: Individualised, with emphasis on avoiding hypoglycaemia in frail patients.

content:
## Scope
Current version. Adds GLP-1 receptor agonists for weight and cardiovascular benefit.

## Diagnosis
Unchanged.

## First-line
Metformin plus early SGLT2 inhibitor where indicated. Consider a GLP-1 receptor agonist for obesity or high cardiovascular risk.

## Renal Caution
eGFR-based review unchanged. During acute kidney injury, hold SGLT2 inhibitors and metformin per sick day rules and the Acute Kidney Injury Pathway.

## Sick Day Rules
Reinforced. Provide every patient a written sick day rules card.

## Targets
Individualised, with emphasis on avoiding hypoglycaemia in frail patients.
citation: Type 2 Diabetes Inpatient and Outpatient Pathway v3.0 (2025), Meridian Health Network — Clinical Standards Office (SYNTHETIC)

title: Acute Ischaemic Stroke Pathway (v1.0, 2016)
issuing_body: Meridian Health Network — Clinical Standards Office (SYNTHETIC)
cross_reference_titles: Anticoagulation and Bleeding Risk Pathway

sections.heading: Scope
sections.text: Hyperacute assessment and reperfusion for adults with suspected acute ischaemic stroke.

sections.heading: Recognition
sections.text: Use the FAST tool (Face, Arm, Speech, Time). Record the time the patient was last known well.

sections.heading: Imaging
sections.text: Perform urgent non-contrast CT brain to exclude haemorrhage before any thrombolysis.

sections.heading: Thrombolysis
sections.text: Offer intravenous alteplase within 4.5 hours of onset if no contraindication, after excluding haemorrhage.

sections.heading: Anticoagulation Interaction
sections.text: Check whether the patient takes anticoagulants, because this affects thrombolysis eligibility. See the Anticoagulation and Bleeding Risk Pathway for reversal.

content:
## Scope
Hyperacute assessment and reperfusion for adults with suspected acute ischaemic stroke.

## Recognition
Use the FAST tool (Face, Arm, Speech, Time). Record the time the patient was last known well.

## Imaging
Perform urgent non-contrast CT brain to exclude haemorrhage before any thrombolysis.

## Thrombolysis
Offer intravenous alteplase within 4.5 hours of onset if no contraindication, after excluding haemorrhage.

## Anticoagulation Interaction
Check whether the patient takes anticoagulants, because this affects thrombolysis eligibility. See the Anticoagulation and Bleeding Risk Pathway for reversal.
citation: Acute Ischaemic Stroke Pathway v1.0 (2016), Meridian Health Network — Clinical Standards Office (SYNTHETIC)

title: Acute Ischaemic Stroke Pathway (v2.0, 2023)
issuing_body: Meridian Health Network — Clinical Standards Office (SYNTHETIC)
cross_reference_titles: Anticoagulation and Bleeding Risk Pathway

sections.heading: Scope
sections.text: Adds mechanical thrombectomy and extended time windows with advanced imaging.

sections.heading: Recognition
sections.text: FAST retained. Pre-hospital large vessel occlusion screening triggers direct transfer to a thrombectomy centre.

sections.heading: Imaging
sections.text: Non-contrast CT plus CT angiography to identify large vessel occlusion. CT perfusion guides extended-window selection.

sections.heading: Thrombolysis
sections.text: Alteplase within 4.5 hours; tenecteplase is an accepted alternative. Thrombectomy is offered up to 24 hours in selected patients with favourable perfusion imaging.

sections.heading: Anticoagulation Interaction
sections.text: For patients on direct oral anticoagulants, follow the Anticoagulation and Bleeding Risk Pathway for reversal agents before any intervention.

content:
## Scope
Adds mechanical thrombectomy and extended time windows with advanced imaging.

## Recognition
FAST retained. Pre-hospital large vessel occlusion screening triggers direct transfer to a thrombectomy centre.

## Imaging
Non-contrast CT plus CT angiography to identify large vessel occlusion. CT perfusion guides extended-window selection.

## Thrombolysis
Alteplase within 4.5 hours; tenecteplase is an accepted alternative. Thrombectomy is offered up to 24 hours in selected patients with favourable perfusion imaging.

## Anticoagulation Interaction
For patients on direct oral anticoagulants, follow the Anticoagulation and Bleeding Risk Pathway for reversal agents before any intervention.
citation: Acute Ischaemic Stroke Pathway v2.0 (2023), Meridian Health Network — Clinical Standards Office (SYNTHETIC)

title: Anticoagulation and Bleeding Risk Pathway (v1.0, 2017)
issuing_body: Meridian Health Network — Clinical Standards Office (SYNTHETIC)
cross_reference_titles: Acute Ischaemic Stroke Pathway
Venous Thromboembolism (VTE) Prevention Pathway

sections.heading: Scope
sections.text: Safe use of anticoagulants and management of bleeding and reversal in adults.

sections.heading: Indications
sections.text: Common indications include atrial fibrillation and venous thromboembolism. Assess stroke risk with CHA2DS2-VASc and bleeding risk with HAS-BLED.

sections.heading: Agents
sections.text: Direct oral anticoagulants (apixaban, rivaroxaban, dabigatran, edoxaban) are preferred over warfarin for most non-valvular atrial fibrillation.

sections.heading: Reversal
sections.text: For dabigatran use idarucizumab. For factor Xa inhibitors use andexanet alfa or prothrombin complex concentrate. For warfarin use vitamin K and prothrombin complex concentrate.

sections.heading: Stroke Link
sections.text: Before stroke thrombolysis, anticoagulant status must be checked; reversal may be required. See the Acute Ischaemic Stroke Pathway.

content:
## Scope
Safe use of anticoagulants and management of bleeding and reversal in adults.

## Indications
Common indications include atrial fibrillation and venous thromboembolism. Assess stroke risk with CHA2DS2-VASc and bleeding risk with HAS-BLED.

## Agents
Direct oral anticoagulants (apixaban, rivaroxaban, dabigatran, edoxaban) are preferred over warfarin for most non-valvular atrial fibrillation.

## Reversal
For dabigatran use idarucizumab. For factor Xa inhibitors use andexanet alfa or prothrombin complex concentrate. For warfarin use vitamin K and prothrombin complex concentrate.

## Stroke Link
Before stroke thrombolysis, anticoagulant status must be checked; reversal may be required. See the Acute Ischaemic Stroke Pathway.
citation: Anticoagulation and Bleeding Risk Pathway v1.0 (2017), Meridian Health Network — Clinical Standards Office (SYNTHETIC)

title: Anticoagulation and Bleeding Risk Pathway (v2.0, 2024)
issuing_body: Meridian Health Network — Clinical Standards Office (SYNTHETIC)
cross_reference_titles: Acute Ischaemic Stroke Pathway
Venous Thromboembolism (VTE) Prevention Pathway

sections.heading: Scope
sections.text: Updated reversal stock guidance and peri-procedural management.

sections.heading: Indications
sections.text: Risk scoring unchanged. Add explicit guidance on anticoagulation in cancer-associated thrombosis.

sections.heading: Agents
sections.text: DOACs remain first-line for most patients. Warfarin retained for mechanical valves and severe kidney impairment.

sections.heading: Reversal
sections.text: Idarucizumab and andexanet alfa stocked in the Emergency Department. Prothrombin complex concentrate remains the fallback for factor Xa inhibitors when andexanet is unavailable.

sections.heading: Peri-procedural
sections.text: Define interruption timing before surgery based on drug and kidney function. Coordinate venous thromboembolism prophylaxis restart with the VTE Prevention Pathway.

content:
## Scope
Updated reversal stock guidance and peri-procedural management.

## Indications
Risk scoring unchanged. Add explicit guidance on anticoagulation in cancer-associated thrombosis.

## Agents
DOACs remain first-line for most patients. Warfarin retained for mechanical valves and severe kidney impairment.

## Reversal
Idarucizumab and andexanet alfa stocked in the Emergency Department. Prothrombin complex concentrate remains the fallback for factor Xa inhibitors when andexanet is unavailable.

## Peri-procedural
Define interruption timing before surgery based on drug and kidney function. Coordinate venous thromboembolism prophylaxis restart with the VTE Prevention Pathway.
citation: Anticoagulation and Bleeding Risk Pathway v2.0 (2024), Meridian Health Network — Clinical Standards Office (SYNTHETIC)

title: Venous Thromboembolism (VTE) Prevention Pathway (v1.0, 2018)
issuing_body: Meridian Health Network — Clinical Standards Office (SYNTHETIC)
cross_reference_titles: Acute Kidney Injury (AKI) Detection and Management Pathway
Anticoagulation and Bleeding Risk Pathway

sections.heading: Scope
sections.text: Risk assessment and prophylaxis to prevent hospital-associated venous thromboembolism.

sections.heading: Risk Assessment
sections.text: Assess every adult admission within 14 hours using the network VTE risk tool, balancing thrombosis risk against bleeding risk.

sections.heading: Prophylaxis
sections.text: Offer low molecular weight heparin to at-risk patients unless contraindicated. Use mechanical prophylaxis when bleeding risk is high.

sections.heading: Renal Adjustment
sections.text: Reduce low molecular weight heparin dose in significant kidney impairment; detection follows the Acute Kidney Injury Pathway.

sections.heading: Restart After Bleeding
sections.text: When anticoagulation is held for bleeding, restart timing is coordinated with the Anticoagulation and Bleeding Risk Pathway.

content:
## Scope
Risk assessment and prophylaxis to prevent hospital-associated venous thromboembolism.

## Risk Assessment
Assess every adult admission within 14 hours using the network VTE risk tool, balancing thrombosis risk against bleeding risk.

## Prophylaxis
Offer low molecular weight heparin to at-risk patients unless contraindicated. Use mechanical prophylaxis when bleeding risk is high.

## Renal Adjustment
Reduce low molecular weight heparin dose in significant kidney impairment; detection follows the Acute Kidney Injury Pathway.

## Restart After Bleeding
When anticoagulation is held for bleeding, restart timing is coordinated with the Anticoagulation and Bleeding Risk Pathway.
citation: Venous Thromboembolism (VTE) Prevention Pathway v1.0 (2018), Meridian Health Network — Clinical Standards Office (SYNTHETIC)

title: Venous Thromboembolism (VTE) Prevention Pathway (v2.0, 2025)
issuing_body: Meridian Health Network — Clinical Standards Office (SYNTHETIC)
cross_reference_titles: Acute Kidney Injury (AKI) Detection and Management Pathway
Anticoagulation and Bleeding Risk Pathway

sections.heading: Scope
sections.text: Adds extended prophylaxis for selected surgical and cancer patients.

sections.heading: Risk Assessment
sections.text: Reassessment now required at 24 hours and whenever clinical condition changes.

sections.heading: Prophylaxis
sections.text: Low molecular weight heparin remains first-line. Direct oral anticoagulants are options for extended prophylaxis after major orthopaedic surgery.

sections.heading: Renal Adjustment
sections.text: Dose reduction thresholds retained; follow the Acute Kidney Injury Pathway for eGFR monitoring.

sections.heading: Restart After Bleeding
sections.text: Coordinate restart with the Anticoagulation and Bleeding Risk Pathway, balancing rebleeding against thrombosis.

content:
## Scope
Adds extended prophylaxis for selected surgical and cancer patients.

## Risk Assessment
Reassessment now required at 24 hours and whenever clinical condition changes.

## Prophylaxis
Low molecular weight heparin remains first-line. Direct oral anticoagulants are options for extended prophylaxis after major orthopaedic surgery.

## Renal Adjustment
Dose reduction thresholds retained; follow the Acute Kidney Injury Pathway for eGFR monitoring.

## Restart After Bleeding
Coordinate restart with the Anticoagulation and Bleeding Risk Pathway, balancing rebleeding against thrombosis.
citation: Venous Thromboembolism (VTE) Prevention Pathway v2.0 (2025), Meridian Health Network — Clinical Standards Office (SYNTHETIC)

title: Pre-eclampsia and Maternal Deterioration Pathway (v1.0, 2017)
issuing_body: Meridian Health Network — Clinical Standards Office (SYNTHETIC)
cross_reference_titles: Adult Sepsis Recognition and Management Pathway
Antimicrobial Stewardship Pathway

sections.heading: Scope
sections.text: Recognition and management of pre-eclampsia and general maternal deterioration in pregnancy and the postnatal period.

sections.heading: Recognition
sections.text: Use the Modified Early Obstetric Warning Score (MEOWS) because normal pregnancy changes vital sign thresholds. Blood pressure of 140/90 mmHg or above with proteinuria suggests pre-eclampsia.

sections.heading: Pre-eclampsia Management
sections.text: Control severe hypertension with labetalol. Use magnesium sulphate to prevent and treat eclamptic seizures.

sections.heading: Maternal Sepsis
sections.text: Maternal sepsis can progress rapidly. Apply the Adult Sepsis Pathway but use MEOWS triggers and pregnancy-safe antibiotics.

sections.heading: Escalation
sections.text: Senior obstetric and anaesthetic review for severe features. Plan timing of delivery as the definitive treatment of pre-eclampsia.

content:
## Scope
Recognition and management of pre-eclampsia and general maternal deterioration in pregnancy and the postnatal period.

## Recognition
Use the Modified Early Obstetric Warning Score (MEOWS) because normal pregnancy changes vital sign thresholds. Blood pressure of 140/90 mmHg or above with proteinuria suggests pre-eclampsia.

## Pre-eclampsia Management
Control severe hypertension with labetalol. Use magnesium sulphate to prevent and treat eclamptic seizures.

## Maternal Sepsis
Maternal sepsis can progress rapidly. Apply the Adult Sepsis Pathway but use MEOWS triggers and pregnancy-safe antibiotics.

## Escalation
Senior obstetric and anaesthetic review for severe features. Plan timing of delivery as the definitive treatment of pre-eclampsia.
citation: Pre-eclampsia and Maternal Deterioration Pathway v1.0 (2017), Meridian Health Network — Clinical Standards Office (SYNTHETIC)

title: Pre-eclampsia and Maternal Deterioration Pathway (v2.0, 2024)
issuing_body: Meridian Health Network — Clinical Standards Office (SYNTHETIC)
cross_reference_titles: Adult Sepsis Recognition and Management Pathway
Antimicrobial Stewardship Pathway

sections.heading: Scope
sections.text: Current version. Strengthens maternal sepsis links and adds postnatal blood pressure follow-up.

sections.heading: Recognition
sections.text: MEOWS retained. Add placental growth factor based testing to support pre-eclampsia diagnosis where available.

sections.heading: Pre-eclampsia Management
sections.text: Labetalol first-line; nifedipine as alternative. Magnesium sulphate unchanged for seizure prevention.

sections.heading: Maternal Sepsis
sections.text: Maternal sepsis must trigger the Adult Sepsis Pathway current version with pregnancy-safe antibiotic selection from the Antimicrobial Stewardship Pathway.

sections.heading: Escalation
sections.text: Severe features require consultant-led review. Provide structured postnatal blood pressure monitoring and community follow-up.

content:
## Scope
Current version. Strengthens maternal sepsis links and adds postnatal blood pressure follow-up.

## Recognition
MEOWS retained. Add placental growth factor based testing to support pre-eclampsia diagnosis where available.

## Pre-eclampsia Management
Labetalol first-line; nifedipine as alternative. Magnesium sulphate unchanged for seizure prevention.

## Maternal Sepsis
Maternal sepsis must trigger the Adult Sepsis Pathway current version with pregnancy-safe antibiotic selection from the Antimicrobial Stewardship Pathway.

## Escalation
Severe features require consultant-led review. Provide structured postnatal blood pressure monitoring and community follow-up.
citation: Pre-eclampsia and Maternal Deterioration Pathway v2.0 (2024), Meridian Health Network — Clinical Standards Office (SYNTHETIC)

title: Delirium Prevention and Management Pathway (v1.0, 2018)
issuing_body: Meridian Health Network — Clinical Standards Office (SYNTHETIC)
cross_reference_titles: Adult Sepsis Recognition and Management Pathway
Acute Kidney Injury (AKI) Detection and Management Pathway
Antimicrobial Stewardship Pathway

sections.heading: Scope
sections.text: Prevention, detection, and management of delirium in hospitalised older adults.

sections.heading: Detection
sections.text: Screen at-risk patients with the 4AT tool on admission and when behaviour changes.

sections.heading: Common Triggers
sections.text: Common precipitants include infection, dehydration, constipation, pain, and medications. Sepsis and acute kidney injury are frequent medical triggers.

sections.heading: Management
sections.text: Treat the underlying cause first. Use non-drug measures: reorientation, sleep, hydration, mobility. Reserve low-dose antipsychotics for severe distress.

sections.heading: Medication Review
sections.text: Review and minimise deliriogenic drugs. For patients with infection, ensure antibiotic doses are correct for kidney function via the Antimicrobial Stewardship Pathway.

content:
## Scope
Prevention, detection, and management of delirium in hospitalised older adults.

## Detection
Screen at-risk patients with the 4AT tool on admission and when behaviour changes.

## Common Triggers
Common precipitants include infection, dehydration, constipation, pain, and medications. Sepsis and acute kidney injury are frequent medical triggers.

## Management
Treat the underlying cause first. Use non-drug measures: reorientation, sleep, hydration, mobility. Reserve low-dose antipsychotics for severe distress.

## Medication Review
Review and minimise deliriogenic drugs. For patients with infection, ensure antibiotic doses are correct for kidney function via the Antimicrobial Stewardship Pathway.
citation: Delirium Prevention and Management Pathway v1.0 (2018), Meridian Health Network — Clinical Standards Office (SYNTHETIC)
